comment.body: Guys, the worst thing you can do is chase. Personally, I was long skew based on Alma’s post, which predicted volatility in the afternoon. Locked in some decent profit. But if you’re trading directionally, wait for the market to actually give you the right setup. What I mean is that,  does it hit your target level, and does price action react to dealer exposure the way you’d normally expect..? If not, that’s a red flag. In that case, just observe, or step away and do something else

comment.body:
Remember, Alma called this for the quarterly OPEX: the market was likely to get pushed down in a pump-and-dump style move due to insider interests. The call-side edge comes from better payoffs thanks to mispriced odds. So if a reversion happens, you can clean up.
That’s why Alma pointed out the spot down, vol down dynamic..it means the vanna flip won’t save you.

comment.body: I’m mainly trying to help those who might not fully grasp what Alma means by call side edge. Basically, calls are underpriced. You can’t really make solid gains on puts intraday because they lack convexity. She also mentioned spot decay.. which she explained as a slow, relatively low rVol price action leading up to March OPEX. You can hedge it, but it’s not the kind of high-reward short trade like when we shorted the tech gamma squeeze last July
comment.raw_body: I’m mainly trying to help those who might not fully grasp what Alma means by call side edge. Basically, calls are underpriced. comment.body:
We’re speculating that if there’s a bounce at March OPEX, we’ll short it..because, like Alma said, that could lead to the real flush, the one that forces the central bank into QE. And yeah, she lays out the daily setup, so for intraday trades, we just watch for deviations to decide if we want to take a call position or sit it out. And if the market isn’t giving the right setup, we don’t force it. You can’t catch em all..
She also posted a screenshot earlier, btw
